attributes.stem:
A 62-year-old man with decompensated alcoholic liver disease arrives to the ward overnight following therapeutic paracentesis in the Emergency Department. He has a medical history of portal hypertension and oesophageal varices and undergoes monthly therapeutic paracentesis. His medications include spironolactone and furosemide.

On examination, he appears comfortable. His pulse rate is 88/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 97% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His abdomen remains moderately distended but is not tense. An ascitic drain was inserted eight hours ago with 5L of straw-coloured fluid collected. His nurse reports no fluid has drained for the past four hours.

Investigations of the ascitic fluid reveal: white cell count 120 × 106/L (<250), protein 28 g/L, albumin 12 g/L.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Perform urgent paracentesis at a different site
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a common situation in the management of ascites where a drain has stopped functioning despite the ongoing presence of ascites. This may be due to a blocked, kinked or mispositioned catheter, or may be due to insertion of the catheter into a loculated ascites pocket, which can be a significant issue in patients undergoing regular paracentesis. The most appropriate action is to remove the drain. There are several important reasons for this: firstly, ascitic drains should generally not remain in situ for more than 6-8 hours due to increasing infection risk. Secondly, a non-draining catheter serves no therapeutic purpose and may increase complications.

Repositioning the drain carries risks of injury and infection. Once an ascitic drain stops functioning, manipulation is not generally recommended. If further drainage is required, a new drain should be inserted under sterile conditions, ideally with ultrasound guidance to reduce complications.

Performing urgent paracentesis at a different site is not necessary. The patient has already had 5L removed and is not severely symptomatic. While a repeat paracentesis may be performed for further relief, there is no urgency to performing this overnight and risking further complications.

Commencing intravenous antibiotics is not indicated as there is no evidence of spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP). The ascitic fluid white cell count is well below the diagnostic threshold of 250 × 106/L.

Taking no action would be inappropriate as the drain has been in situ for eight hours and is no longer functioning. Leaving the drain in place increases infection risk without providing therapeutic benefit.

Best practice for ascitic drainage includes using ultrasound guidance where possible, maintaining strict sterile technique, administering albumin during significant drainage, placing a limit on total volume drained to prevent post-paracentesis circulatory dysfunction, and removing drains once they stop functioning or after 6-8 hours, noting that most centres will have a local protocol which should be followed and clearly documented.
learningPoints: Ascitic drains in patients with decompensated cirrhosis should generally be removed after no longer than 8 hours or once they stop draining to reduce the risk of infection
references: Harvey JJ, Prentice R, George J. Diagnostic and therapeutic abdominal paracentesis. Med J Aust. 2023 Jan 16;218(1):18-21. Available at: https://www.mja.com.au/journal/2023/218/1/diagnostic-and-therapeutic-abdominal-paracentesis
